page: Vultr vcg-a40-96c-480g-192vram is a GPU-accelerated server instance within the Cloud GPU family. The system features 96 shared Intel x86_64 vCPUs paired with 480.0 GB of system memory, yielding a ratio of 5.0 GB of RAM per core. Graphics and parallel computing acceleration are driven by four NVIDIA Ampere A40 GPUs, providing a combined total of 192 GB of VRAM. Local storage consists of a 1400 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

The processing architecture relies on shared Intel vCPUs, which may introduce resource contention compared to dedicated cores. Additionally, the CPU configuration lacks extra availability for advanced instruction sets, including Advanced Vector Extensions (AVX, AVX512), Advanced Matrix Extensions (AMX), cryptographic acceleration (AES, SHA), and virtualization flags. This hardware profile suggests that workloads heavily dependent on specialized CPU-bound vector instructions or hardware-assisted cryptography will experience limitations, shifting the primary computational burden to the onboard NVIDIA GPUs.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers high resource density by bundling substantial GPU memory and system RAM on a shared CPU platform. This configuration is designed for workloads that prioritize massive parallel GPU compute over dedicated single-core CPU performance. Appropriate workloads include large-scale machine learning model training, multi-GPU deep learning inference, data science pipelines, and complex 3D rendering tasks that leverage the Ampere architecture.
description: Vultr vcg-a40-96c-480g-192vram is a GPU-accelerated server in the Cloud GPU family, configured with 96 shared Intel x86_64 vCPUs and 480.0 GB of system memory. The hardware profile features four NVIDIA Ampere A40 GPUs delivering 192 GB of total VRAM, alongside a 1400 GB NVMe SSD for local storage. A key architectural tradeoff is the shared CPU allocation and the absence of advanced CPU instruction sets such as AVX-512, AMX, and AES. This resource density makes the instance qualitatively cost-efficient for parallel processing tasks rather than CPU-bound operations. It is suited for multi-GPU deep learning, large-scale machine learning inference, data science workloads, and 3D rendering.
